title: #eng-oncall: 1740169022
preview:
Tomáš Novák: During the Sev1 we had a pretty big runbook gap for TP hangs. We did the right things eventually (drain/replace replicas + cap concurrency) but we were basically guessing. Can we capture what we *wish* the runbook had?

Rafael Mendes: +1. In incident channel folks kept asking “what do I check to confirm it’s a TP stall vs just slow traffic” and we didn’t have a crisp checklist.

Aisha Bello: Also no single dashboard panel to point at. We bounced between request timeouts, GPU util, and random logs.

Tomáš Novák: Concrete gaps I noticed:
1) What commands to run *immediately* on a suspected wedged TP replica
2) Which metrics confirm heartbeat/collective stall
3) What mitigations are safe (and in what order)
4) When to page runtime vs keep in SRE lane

Noah Patel: Agree. For TP stalls the biggest distinction is “comms/collective progress stopped” vs “scheduler starvation/backpressure causing no progress”. We need both signals.

Hiro Tanaka: From kernels side, we should explicitly say: if NCCL collectives are stuck, restarting the pod is often the only recovery. But we should capture logs *before* restart if possible.

Rafael Mendes: What should oncall run right now (today), given current tooling?

Omar El-Sayed: Minimal triage checklist (current state):
- Identify replica/pod(s) correlated with timeouts.
- Check for TP progress logs / per-rank heartbeat (we don’t have consistent metrics yet, but some logs exist).
- Pull NCCL warnings if enabled.

But yeah… not copy/pasteable.

Jacob Stein: We can at least document a “grab bag” of commands. Something like:

1) Find worst offenders (pods) for timeouts
```
# pick namespace/cluster accordingly
kubectl -n runtime get pods -l app=redwood-runtime -o wide
kubectl -n runtime top pod | head
```
2) Tail runtime logs for stall hints
```
kubectl -n runtime logs -f <pod> -c runtime --since=10m | egrep -i "tp|nccl|collective|watchdog|stall|timeout"
```
3) Quick GPU snapshot (if we have node access / daemonset)
```
# via debug container / node shell
nvidia-smi
nvidia-smi dmon -s pucm -d 2 -c 5
```

We should decide the supported path (we don’t always want node access in runbook).

Aisha Bello: From metrics side (what we can reliably query in Prom right now):
- `request_timeout_total{route=...,model=...}` broken down by `pod`
- `runtime_queue_depth` (coarse)
- `gpu_utilization` / `gpu_memory_used_bytes`

But we’re missing: per-rank heartbeat gap, in-flight collective counters, and “stall reason”. That’s what ENG-4823/PR-21834 are adding, but runbook should mention “if available in >=1.19”.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler-specific: when we see long prefill waves, decode can starve and then collectives get out of sync. We need a specific “scheduler starvation suspicion” indicator:
- queue depth growing while GPU util oscillates
- decode iterations not advancing (we might only infer via token output stall)

Noah Patel: Can we standardize the “is it wedged?” test for streaming? During the incident we used “no tokens streamed for >N seconds” but N varied.

Rafael Mendes: For oncall we need a decision tree:
- If customer-facing streaming timeouts increasing AND same pods implicated AND tokens stop streaming -> treat as probable TP stall.
- If GPU util high but tokens slow -> capacity/overload.
- If GPU util low and queue depth high -> scheduler/backpressure.

Tomáš Novák: Mitigations we used (and should document):
1) Drain suspected replicas out of service (so they stop taking requests)
2) Restart pods to clear wedge
3) Temporarily reduce concurrency / batch limits for TP routes
4) If still ongoing: reroute to non-TP or smaller model variant (if policy allows)

Connor O'Brien: From release/ops standpoint, please call out feature flags / config knobs explicitly. During the incident someone asked “what’s the safest knob to turn” and we had to dig.

Hiro Tanaka: Knobs we’ve used as mitigations:
- cap max concurrent requests per TP replica
- disable CUDA graph capture for TP (if enabled)
- force extra stream sync path (perf hit but safer)

But we should be careful: some of these are only in certain versions / behind flags.

Sean Gallagher: +1 to version scoping. Runbook should have “Runtime version <1.19” vs “>=1.19 with watchdog” sections.

Aisha Bello: For alerts: we should add a runbook section “what alert fired / what panel to look at”:
- high `request_timeout_total` correlated to `pod`
- if/when we have it: `tp_heartbeat_gap_seconds` and `tp_stall_reason_total{reason=...}`

Tomáš Novák: Immediate follow-ups (today/tomorrow) we can action without waiting for new code:
- [ ] I’ll draft a runbook page with current commands + mitigation order, with big caveats on node access.
- [ ] Aisha: can you add a temporary dashboard note/panel that shows timeouts by pod for TP routes? Even if it’s janky.
- [ ] Rafael: can you add a short “when to drain” guideline for SREs (and ensure we have permissions documented)?

Rafael Mendes: Yep. Also adding explicit “collect logs before restart” note:
- last 10m runtime logs
- any NCCL debug snippet if enabled
- pod description/events

Jacob Stein: Add to commands:
```
kubectl -n runtime describe pod <pod>
# capture node name + events
```
And if we have the debug endpoint:
```
curl -s http://<pod-ip>:<port>/debug/runtime_state | head
```
(we should verify endpoint name; don’t want to publish fake commands.)

Omar El-Sayed: For runtime_state endpoint: it exists in some envs but not standardized. For now runbook should say “if `/_debug/runtime` is enabled in this cluster” and link to internal docs.

Sofia Ivanova: Also add guidance: don’t keep hammering a wedged replica with retries. If you see repeated timeouts from same pod, drain it fast. Retries pile up and make it worse.

Noah Patel: +1. Put this in bold. TP stalls tend to be sticky; letting load accumulate increases blast radius.

Sean Gallagher: Action item: once PR-21834/TP heartbeat lands, SRE needs to update the runbook to “preferred signals” and deprecate the guesswork.

Tomáš Novák: Noted. I’ll make the runbook draft explicitly call out “today: heuristics” and “post-1.19: use heartbeat + collective counters”.

Aisha Bello: Re: default thresholds, we can put placeholders:
- “If no tokens streamed for >60s (configurable by model), suspect stall”
- “If timeouts spike >X/min on same replica, drain”
We can refine later.

Rafael Mendes: Please also include the escalation checklist:
- page Runtime (Kernels/Scheduling) if >2 replicas wedge in 15m or if drain/restart doesn’t recover
- open INT helpdesk if we need temporary extra NCCL logging

Tomáš Novák: Got it. I’ll link to INT-2219 template once it’s created/updated.

Connor O'Brien: When you draft, include the exact Slack channels to coordinate: `#incidents`, `#eng-runtime`, `#eng-sre`, and `#eng-releases` if we’re changing flags.

Tomáš Novák: Will do. I’m going to start a Confluence page under eng-serving-runtime/runbooks and we can iterate. I’ll drop a link here once it’s up.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. This is exactly the kind of “paper cut” that turns into hours during a Sev1. Let’s get it into the oncall rotation notes too.

Tomáš Novák: :thumbsup: I’ll post draft EOD.

title: #eng-oncall: 1739827209
preview:
Miles Thornton: looking for quick feedback from folks who’ve used the v2 beta cards this week. are they too verbose? too sparse? what would you change for “first 5 min” context?

Aisha Bello: i like having it all, but the saturation card is a wall of text in Slack. i end up scrolling past it to get to the actual incident thread.

Jacob Stein: +1. the “config diff” is the worst offender. in one incident it dumped like 80 lines (even with redaction) and it drowned out the human updates.

Devon Price: yep Slack is also not super forgiving. Block Kit sections start getting gnarly once we’re > ~3k chars. we’re doing some truncation but it’s not opinionated enough.

Rafael Mendes: for oncall: default should be *summary only* with one-click expand. i want to know:
- did anything deploy in last ~60m for this service/region
- did config/routing change recently
- are we saturated (gpu/queue)
then links.

Mina Haddad: agreed. also the routing bits should highlight only the stuff that affects traffic decisions:
- fallback enabled/disabled
- model pin changes
- weight shifts / route policy changes
- quota / rate limit changes
not every field in the config tree.

Logan Wright: saturation card: prefer “top 3 signals” + whether they crossed threshold + trend arrow. raw numbers for everything is noisy.

Miles Thornton: can you be specific about “top 3”? which ones do you actually look at?

Logan Wright: in practice:
1) queue depth / admission control (if it’s growing, that’s the story)
2) gpu util (but only meaningful w/ queue)
3) kv cache pressure / evictions (if we’re churning)
then link to the saturation overview dashboard. everything else can be behind “details”.

Aisha Bello: + include error budget burn link per region. i keep hopping between 2-3 dashboards to confirm it’s not just a noisy p95.

Chloe Martin: deep link wise: please make sure the links land me in Console with the right filters already set. biggest pain today is “dashboard link” opens global view and i lose 60 seconds clicking around.

Jacob Stein: + the links should lock the time window to incident start-ish. like “last 30m” or “since thread created”. if it opens “last 24h” it’s not helpful.

Devon Price: we can do both:
- default time window = `thread_ts - 30m to now`
- include a secondary link “open last 24h” for context

Mina Haddad: another thing: truncation rules need to be deterministic. i saw a diff where the interesting change got cut off but a bunch of unchanged metadata made it in.

Devon Price: what’s the ideal truncation heuristic?

Mina Haddad: for config diff:
- show only changed paths (no surrounding context)
- cap at e.g. 10 changed paths
- if > 10, show “+N more changes” and force modal
- within a path, show only old/new scalar values, don’t dump full nested objects

Jacob Stein: + categorize changes. like:
“Routing policy: 2 changes”
“Feature flags: 1 change”
“Quotas: 3 changes”
then list the specific ones under each. easier to scan.

Aisha Bello: and give a redaction warning that’s not scary but clear. right now it says “REDACTED” a lot and ppl assume the bot is broken.

Miles Thornton: +1. people asked me if it was failing because half the diff says `[REDACTED]`.

Devon Price: we can adjust copy to: “Sensitive values hidden. Use break-glass to view full diff (audited).”

Rafael Mendes: please don’t push break-glass in the happy path. most incidents don’t need it. just make sure summary contains the non-sensitive part.

Chloe Martin: deep links i want per card:
- Deploys: link to deploy timeline (service + region + last 2h) + link to PR/commit
- Config diff: link to “Config changes” view for that tenant/env (but safe) + link to request elevated access
- Saturation: link to “Capacity & saturation” dashboard with pool/region preselected
- Errors: link to “SLO burn” + “Top 5 error routes”

Logan Wright: + for saturation: add a “copy query” action if possible. i sometimes want to run the exact PromQL/NRQL with tweaks.

Devon Price: Slack action buttons can do “Copy to clipboard” only in modals (not always), but we can show a code block with the query + “open in Grafana” link.

incident-bot: :information_source: FYI Slack message payload limit hit; long diffs will be moved to a modal automatically.

Jacob Stein: lol that’s what we want :thumbsup:

Miles Thornton: what about the “recent deploys” card — is it too sparse now?

Aisha Bello: deploy card is mostly good. only tweak: if there were no deploys, say “No deploys detected for service X in region Y in last 60m” (explicit) so i don’t wonder if it failed to query.

Rafael Mendes: + add canary status *only if* canary exists. otherwise it’s confusing. i saw “Canary: N/A” and wasted time thinking there was supposed to be one.

Devon Price: got it. we’ll hide canary row when not applicable.

Mina Haddad: also: if there *was* a deploy but it’s in staged rollout, show rollout phase clearly (ex: “Phase 1/3 - 10% EU-West only”). that’s actually the key triage clue.

Miles Thornton: cool. summarizing proposed changes:
- summary-first cards, details behind modal
- config diff truncation by changed paths, cap 10, group by category
- saturation: highlight queue/gpu/kv w/ thresholds + trend; details behind modal
- deep links must prefilter service/region/tenant and time window anchored to thread
- explicit “no data” vs “query failed” messaging

Dylan Brooks: +1 from tooling side. please also keep card height consistent. if saturation explodes the layout it pushes human updates below the fold.

Devon Price: i’ll cut a ticket for:
1) deterministic truncation + grouping
2) link parameterization w/ thread_ts window
3) “no data” vs “error” states

Miles Thornton: thanks all. keep feedback coming esp. with screenshots from real incidents.

Aisha Bello: will do. i’ll drop a snippet next time. :pray:

title: #eng-oncall: 1739802110
preview:
Dev Patel: quick q for whoever’s around — for the quant profile kill-switch, what *exactly* happens when we flip it? does it hard-disable profile application even if route config has `quantization_profile_ref` set?

Dev Patel: also in a rollback, what are the 2-3 things oncall should check to confirm we’re actually back to baseline?

Connor O'Brien: yep, kill-switch is a global override. when enabled, policy resolution returns “disabled” regardless of route-level `quantization_profile_ref` (even explicit profile IDs).

Connor O'Brien: mental model:
1) Route config says “default” / “none” / “profile:<id>”
2) Policy engine evaluates compatibility + tenant overrides
3) THEN global kill-switch is applied last and forces disable

Aisha Bello: +1. dashboards should show `quant_profile_applied_rate` drop to ~0 and `quant_profile_resolution_outcome{outcome="disabled"}` spike.

Benji Okafor: name of the flag in config is `quant_profiles.global_disable=true` (control plane reads it, cached ~30s; runtime sees it via resolved policy).

Dev Patel: ok, so even if customer keeps toggling in Console, it won’t take effect while kill-switch is on?

Ethan Park: correct. Console will still write the setting + audit it, but resolved policy will return disabled. (Console shows a banner “Global disable active” in prod once the backend returns the override bit.)

Chloe Martin: re Console behavior: user sees their route setting saved, but “Effective state” will show Disabled + reason = GLOBAL_KILL_SWITCH.

Mina Haddad: for rollback runbook, order we’ve been recommending:
1) disable profile at the impacted route(s) if it’s isolated (fast + minimal blast radius)
2) if broad / unknown scope, flip global kill-switch
3) follow up: remove/cleanup route refs once stable

Ravi Menon: also note: compatibility fallback vs kill-switch disable are distinct in logs/metrics. fallback happens when policy says “profile requested” but incompatible; kill-switch says “no profile allowed”.

Dev Patel: what do I check per-request to confirm baseline? is there a trace tag?

Noah Patel: runtime logs/traces include `quant_profile_id` + `quant_profile_resolution`.
- applied: `quant_profile_resolution=applied` and `quant_profile_id=<profile>`
- fallback: `quant_profile_resolution=fallback` (will often still include requested id + fallback_reason)
- disabled: `quant_profile_resolution=disabled` and `quant_profile_id=` (empty)

Noah Patel: in Honeycomb/Tracing you can filter span fields:
`quant_profile_resolution != "applied"` to verify we’re not applying.

Aisha Bello: quick checklist we’ve been using during rollback:

title: #eng-oncall: 1739799088
preview:
Miles Thornton: heads up for next week’s on-call rotations — we’re going to start seeing more nodes sit in `Validating` as provision-to-ready gates roll out. want to align on: what “Validating” *means*, when it’s “stuck”, when to quarantine, and who to page (Infra vs Platform).

Aisha Bello: +1. I’m updating the alert runbook and need crisp thresholds. right now folks see “Validating” and assume kube is broken.

Rafael Mendes: can we treat this thread as the on-call cheat sheet? I’ll paste into #eng-sre-runbooks after.

Vanessa Ortiz: yep. quick definition (per our state machine):
- `Provisioning`: instance boot + join steps (kubelet, runtime config, labels/taints)
- `Validating`: node has joined cluster, agent is running validations (gpu health, driver parity, net/storage probes, NCCL smoke, perf canary)
- `Ready`: validations passed, provisioning taint removed, node can be added to serving pool
- `Quarantined`: node failed repeatedly or hit a “hard fail” and is removed from rotation / cordoned

Jordan Lee: minor nuance: `Validating` is authoritative from Fleet Readiness Service (control plane), not “whatever kube says”. kube can be Ready while we’re still in Validating.

Miles Thornton: what should on-call interpret as “Validating but fine” vs “Validating stuck”?

Harish Reddy: practical ops view:
- Validating < ~10 min is normal for fresh nodes (esp NCCL + perf canary)
- if Validating > 25–30 min for a batch, something’s wrong (usually agent not running / NCCL hanging / network flake)

Aisha Bello: can we codify “stuck” threshold by step? the dashboard is step breakdown now.

Sergio Costa: dashboard has per-step durations. rule of thumb from staging:
- GPU health + driver parity: should be <2–3 min
- kube join checks: <5 min
- NCCL smoke: 3–8 min normally (longer if retries)
- perf canary: 2–5 min
if a single step is >15 min, that’s a smell.

Allison Grant: + reminder: look at step breakdown, not just overall Validating. overall can look scary but it’s usually one step timing out.

Miles Thornton: ok, decision tree for pager:
1) node stuck Validating — where do we look first?

Benji Okafor: first check Fleet Readiness Service view (not kube):
- internal endpoint: `/internal/fleet/readiness/nodes?pool_id=…` (or via console)
- it will show `current_state`, `last_transition_at`, and `failure_reason` if any.
If the service is returning stale state / 5xx, that’s Platform.

Ethan Park: +1. if you can’t fetch readiness state or transitions aren’t persisting, page Platform Control Plane.

Vanessa Ortiz: if readiness service is fine, check validation artifacts pointer (it stores where logs are). usually points at validation-agent logs or perf-canary run.

Rafael Mendes: what’s the “fast path” check if you’re mid-incident?

Harish Reddy: for a single node:
- `kubectl describe node <node>` — confirm taint `redwood.ai/provisioning=true:NoSchedule` still present
- `kubectl -n fleet-validation get pods -o wide | grep <node>` — validation agent running?
- if no agent pod scheduled → Infra (daemonset/helm/cluster config)
- if pod crashlooping → Infra (image/agent) unless error looks like authz to readiness API (then Platform)

Aisha Bello: can you paste example commands? people always ask.

Harish Reddy: sure:
`kubectl describe node ip-10-..` (look for taints + conditions)
`kubectl -n fleet-validation get pods -o wide --field-selector spec.nodeName=<node>`
`kubectl -n fleet-validation logs ds/node-validation-agent -c agent --since=30m | tail -200`
`kubectl -n perf-canary logs deploy/post-provision-canary --since=30m`

Sergio Costa: if it’s *many* nodes, start from Grafana:
- Provision-to-ready overview (time-to-ready + state counts)
- Step breakdown heatmap (step_name x region/gpu)
- Quarantine counts
Then drill into logs for the dominant failure.

Miles Thornton: quarantine criteria — when can on-call quarantine vs retry?

Vanessa Ortiz: policy we’re using in rollout:
- soft failures (timeouts, transient net checks): auto-retry up to 3 attempts. on-call can trigger “retry validation” once if it looks like a one-off.
- hard failures (GPU Xid, persistent ECC, driver mismatch, missing NVLink fabric, NCCL all-reduce consistently failing): quarantine immediately.

Rafael Mendes: can we define hard-fail signals more concretely?

Vanessa Ortiz: hard fail examples:
- `nvidia-smi` reports GPU fallen off bus
- Xid codes in dmesg repeated during validation
- ECC error rate above threshold (agent marks `gpu_health=fail`)
- driver/CUDA mismatch vs golden image channel (parity check fails)
- NCCL smoke test fails *twice* with same topology error (not a timeout)

Allison Grant: also: if validation failures spike right after an image channel change, don’t quarantine the world — consider rollback first. that’s Infra + Release Eng.

Connor O'Brien: +1. if you see a sharp step failure increase correlated w/ `image_channel=candidate` rollout, page Release Eng + Infra before mass quarantine. we can pull the kill switch.

Aisha Bello: paging guidance: Infra vs Platform vs SRE?

Miles Thornton: SRE is the *entry point* if an alert fires, but ownership is Infra/Platform depending on failure class. I’d like explicit “page X when Y” bullets.

Ethan Park: Platform page conditions:
- Fleet Readiness Service 5xx / latency
- state transitions not being recorded (events received but DB write failing)
- authz failures calling readiness mutation endpoints (403/401 in agent)
- mismatch between “validation says pass” but state never leaves Validating (control-plane logic / state machine)

Vanessa Ortiz: Infra page conditions:
- validation agent not scheduled / daemonset broken
- image bake regression (driver install, containerd/kubelet config)
- kube join failures (node never appears, or flaps NodeReady)
- NCCL failures that look like fabric/network config (not readiness API)
- provisioning orchestrator step failures / retries exhausted

Sergio Costa: “who owns perf canary?” it’s kinda infra-ish but runs app-ish code.

Vanessa Ortiz: perf canary failures that are “can’t pull image / can’t reach model endpoint / node networking” → Infra. if perf canary is failing because readiness service says “not allowed” or metadata missing (pool id, tokens) → Platform.

Rafael Mendes: quarantine vs page: if we have capacity shortfall risk, do we page immediately?

Harish Reddy: if quarantines are causing pool below committed capacity, page Infra + Capacity Ops immediately (Jamal usually). otherwise, quarantine is ok as long as replacement nodes are coming.

Aisha Bello: I’ll add note: “Quarantine reduces capacity; if pool utilization >80% and quarantine count rising, treat as customer-impacting risk.”

Incident Bot: Reminder: If customer impact is suspected, open an incident in #incidents and assign an IC/Comms. Template: https://incident-bot.redwood/runbooks/provisioning-failure

Miles Thornton: what about “Validating stuck but kube looks healthy” scenarios? folks get confused b/c NodeReady=true.

Jordan Lee: that’s expected. kube readiness != inference readiness. Validating is the gate. until the provisioning taint is removed + readiness state is Ready, the node should not join serving pools.

Benji Okafor: and if you see kube NodeReady=true but readiness state hasn’t moved in >30 min *and* no new validation artifacts are being written, that’s usually agent-heartbeat missing (Infra) or artifact ingestion failing (Platform). readiness service will show “last_agent_heartbeat_at” in the detail view — use that.

Aisha Bello: do we have a rule: heartbeat stale => Infra?

Ethan Park: mostly yes. if heartbeat missing because agent can’t auth to readiness (token expired / 401) that’s Platform-ish, but the symptom is in agent logs.

Vanessa Ortiz: practical: if `last_agent_heartbeat_at` >10m old → check agent pod logs first. if it’s crashlooping or not scheduled → Infra. if logs show `403` to readiness endpoint → Platform.

Rafael Mendes: ok, summarizing for on-call:
- “Validating” normal up to ~10–15m, investigate >25–30m
- Look at readiness service detail: failure_reason, current_step, last heartbeat
- Use step breakdown dashboard to find dominant stuck step
- Quarantine immediately on hard GPU health / driver parity / repeated NCCL topology errors
- Page Platform for readiness service issues / authz / state machine stuck with validations passed
- Page Infra for agent/daemonset, image, kube join, NCCL/fabric, orchestrator retries

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: I’ll bake this into the on-call doc + link dashboards/runbook.

Connor O'Brien: can you also add “if correlated with image channel change, consider rollback / disable flag”?

Vanessa Ortiz: yep. and for next week rollout, we’re limiting to us-east A100; if on-call sees weirdness outside that scope, likely unrelated.

Miles Thornton: thanks all. if anyone has concrete error signatures (“NCCL WARN NET/Socket” etc) drop them here and I’ll add to runbook.

Harish Reddy: common ones from last week:
- `ncclSystemError: Socket recv failed` → usually flaky fabric; retry once, then Infra if repeat
- `CUDA driver version is insufficient` → image/driver mismatch → quarantine + Infra
- `readiness-api 403 forbidden: missing pool:write` → Platform authz/role mapping

Benji Okafor: + if you see `failed to persist validation artifact: timeout` in readiness svc logs, page Platform — that blocks transitions even when validations pass.

Aisha Bello: noted. I’ll post final cheat sheet in this channel once updated.

title: #eng-oncall: 1739563228
preview:
Rafael Mendes: on-call handoff for audit exporter incident from earlier (lag spike / downstream 429s). capturing what to watch + toggles. cc @Aisha Bello (taking primary)

Redwood Incident Bot: Incident INC-4471 status changed: MITIGATED → MONITORING

Rafael Mendes: TL;DR
- root cause: Splunk HEC rate-limiting (429) on 2 beta tenants → retries/backoff built up queue → export lag peaked ~38m
- mitigation: paused Splunk adapter for affected tenants + reduced batch size + verified no DLQ growth
- current: lag back < 5m; retry rate normalizing

Rafael Mendes: Dashboards to keep open tonight:
1) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Overview (lag + throughput)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-overview
   Panels to watch:
   - "p95 export lag (by destination)"
   - "events exported/sec (by tenant)"
   - "checkpoint age"
2) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Destinations
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-destinations
   Panels:
   - "adapter HTTP status codes" (look for sustained 429/403)
   - "retry attempts" + "backoff seconds"
3) Queue/Storage dashboard (exporter internal)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-queues
   Panels:
   - "pending batches" (should not trend up)
   - "DLQ depth" (should be ~0; anything non-zero means investigate)

Aisha Bello: got it. what are the paging thresholds we’re using right now?

Rafael Mendes: from SRE draft (we used these in the incident):
- page if p95 export lag > 15m for 10m (any destination) OR > 30m for 5m (critical)
- page if delivery success rate < 99.5% over 30m (per adapter)
- ticket-only if 429s elevated but lag < 10m
(Sean can confirm if we’re tweaking, but this is what alert rules are set to as of today)

Sean Gallagher: confirming. alert rules match. note: we loosened 429 warning to ticket-only after the noise last week.

Rafael Mendes: Toggles / controls (use sparingly; prefer tenant-scoped first):
A) Pause exports per destination type (safe):
- control-plane admin: `POST /internal/audit-exports/{tenant_id}/pause` with body `{"destination":"splunk"}`
- UI (Console → Audit Exports → Actions → Pause) works if you have the SRE role
This stops new sends but keeps checkpoints; lag metric will stop increasing but backlog remains.

B) Global kill switch (last resort):
- feature flag: `siem_audit_export_beta_enabled` (tenant allowlist). DO NOT disable globally unless we’re melting down.
- if you must, flip via internal admin tool: https://admin.redwood.internal/flags/siem_audit_export_beta_enabled

C) Backpressure safety / auto-pause:

- when it triggers, you’ll see alert "destination auto-paused" + log line `AUTO_PAUSE_MAX_LAG`.

Rafael Mendes: "exports temporarily paused due to downstream rate limiting; no audit events deleted; once resumed exporter will backfill from last checkpoint; duplicates possible (at-least-once)". don’t promise exact timestamps.

Logan Wright: adding: if customer asks "missing events", point them to dedup via `event_id` on their side. at-least-once semantics.

1) Identify adapter causing it:
   - check "p95 export lag by destination" + "HTTP status codes"
2) If 429s from Splunk:

   - reduce pressure first (tenant pause Splunk) rather than global
3) If 403s from Datadog:
   - usually API key scope / wrong site (datadoghq.com vs datadoghq.eu). see SUP-18497 notes.
4) If S3 errors:
   - look for `AccessDenied` or KMS issues; often rotated role trust or KMS key policy.

- exporter logs (prod):

title: #eng-oncall: 1738777444
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to nail down restore drill cadence + paging policy for Private. Right now the staging drill failures are noisy and folks are getting paged at 2am for non-prod :/

Sean Gallagher: Proposal: automated restore drill in staging 1x/week. Production drill cadence 1x/month (or quarterly?) but *not* automated at first. Thoughts?

Neha Kapoor: +1 on weekly staging. For prod, I’d start w/ quarterly until we have confidence + customer comms templates.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. Also: who owns responding when the drill fails? If we page “eng-oncall” then it needs to be actionable + have clear runbook.

Sergio Costa: From dashboards side, the metrics we’re emitting (`restore_drill_success`, `restore_drill_duration_seconds`, `restore_drill_last_success_ts`) are ready. We can alert on “no successful drill in N days” vs “single failure” to reduce flapping.

Allison Grant: +1. Alerting should be based on SLO semantics:
- staging: notify (Slack) on failure, page only if error budget burn / repeated failures
- prod: page on failure (since it implies DR readiness regression)

Talia Benson: Can we align to ENG-9827 (restore drill automation) + ENG-9826 (alerts) acceptance? PM asks for a concrete supportable policy.

Miles Thornton: Cadence suggestion by risk:
- Staging: weekly drill, but treat as canary for tooling
- Prod: monthly is ambitious; quarterly is fine IF you also do “backup integrity checks” daily
Also important: distinguish “control plane restore validation” vs “etcd snapshot restore” drill.

Benji Okafor: FWIW the drill currently fails if CRDs aren’t present / ordering issues (we saw this in the on-prem case). If we page on single staging failure we’ll be paging a lot until we harden the restore ordering.

Sean Gallagher: OK. So: staging weekly, but page only if it’s persistently failing (or no success for X days). Non-prod: gate pages to business hours.

Sergio Costa: We can do business-hours gating in Alertmanager via `time_intervals` and route based on `env=staging` label.

Sergio Costa: Rough sketch:
```yaml
route:
  receiver: slack-eng-oncall
  routes:
  - matchers:
    - env="staging"
    receiver: slack-eng-oncall
    routes:
    - matchers:
      - severity="page"
      receiver: pagerduty-eng-oncall
      active_time_intervals: [business-hours]
  - matchers:
    - env="prod"
    receiver: pagerduty-eng-oncall

time_intervals:
- name: business-hours
  time_intervals:
  - weekdays: [monday:friday]
    times:
    - start_time: "09:00"
      end_time: "17:00"
    location: "America/Los_Angeles"
```

Allison Grant: That’s fine. Also please avoid paging on “single run failed” for staging. Alert should be “no successful restore drill in 14 days” *and* “backup job success < 99% over 7d” (or similar). Otherwise maintenance windows will trigger.

Neha Kapoor: +1 and we should incorporate maintenance window silences (label `maintenance=true`) for planned cluster upgrades.

Sean Gallagher: Who gets paged if prod monthly/quarterly drill fails? SRE oncall, or “Private Deployments oncall”? We don’t have the latter.

Rafael Mendes: Until we have a dedicated rota, it should be SRE oncall (PagerDuty: eng-oncall). But the runbook needs clear “handoff to control plane eng” criteria.

Talia Benson: Can we define:
- PagerDuty “eng-oncall” for prod drill failure
- Slack-only for staging drill failure
- If staging has no success in 14d, then page during biz hours (to force cleanup)

Miles Thornton: That’s a reasonable compromise. The “no success in 14d” alert is basically “DR readiness signal is stale.”

Benji Okafor: For staging weekly drill — can we schedule it Tue/Thu mid-day? Lots of Monday deploy churn.

Sean Gallagher: Works for me. Let’s do Tue 11am PT for staging drill.

Sergio Costa: Cool. I’ll update the alert definitions + routing.
- `RestoreDrillFailed` => Slack (staging)
- `RestoreDrillStale` (no success in 14d) => PagerDuty only during biz hours (staging)
- `RestoreDrillFailed` => PagerDuty 24/7 (prod)

Allison Grant: Please add runbook links into alert annotations (Confluence page + “what to check first”: bucket permissions, KMS reachability, CRDs present).

Neha Kapoor: Also: let’s set severity conventions:
- staging: `severity=warn` (Slack) / `severity=page` (stale only)
- prod: `severity=page`

Alertmanager: [bot] Reminder: Changes to routing rules require PR + approval from #eng-sre-configs reviewers.

PagerDuty: [bot] Service “eng-oncall” has an established noise budget. Consider bundling staging pages under a separate service if volume increases.

Sean Gallagher: Action items:
1) Sergio to implement Alertmanager gating + updated alerts (include biz hours for staging pages)
2) Sean to update oncall runbook w/ “restore drill triage” steps + escalation criteria
3) Talia to capture decision in ENG-9827/9826 as acceptance criteria
:thumbsup:?

title: #eng-oncall: 1738265401
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Dropping this here for on-call review: rollback runbook for optimization configs is basically done, but we need to decide *who* can trigger rollback during Sev events.

Sean Gallagher: Runbook draft says “Optimization Change Operator” but that’s too narrow for Sev1 at 3am. We need something that’s safe + auditable.

Aisha Bello: +1. If p99 is on fire after a config apply, SRE oncall needs a deterministic way to undo without waiting for someone w/ a niche role.

Paula Mendes: From product side: we told a couple enterprise prospects “one-click rollback,” but we also promised strong access controls. So “anyone” is a no.

Jordan Lee: RBAC currently has `optimization_config.rollback` permission planned under the “optimization change operator” bundle (PM-902 / PR-20070). We can add a second role for SRE oncall / incident commander.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: rollback is a production change. I’m okay with SRE oncall having rollback *if*:
- scoped to Hosted/Dedicated only (control plane managed)
- requires reason + incident ID (or at least change ticket)
- emits audit event w/ request_id + actor + previous_version + target_version + scope
- optional: 2-person rule for Sev0/Sev1 (approve + execute)

Ethan Park: On the API side we can require `reason` + `actor_context` and propagate `incident_id` into audit payload. Console already planned to do “type-to-confirm”.

Chloe Martin: Console UX can enforce “type ROLLBACK + tenant/model/region scope” and require selecting a prior known-good version. But RBAC is the real gate.

Connor O'Brien: Release eng concern: we’ll get paged for latency regressions during release trains too. If rollback requires a special person, it’ll slow MTTR. But also we don’t want casual rollbacks for non-incidents.

Naomi Feldman: Can we distinguish:
- Sev0/Sev1 incident mitigation (fast path)
- non-incident change (normal approvals)

Jordan Lee: Yes. RBAC can’t read “incident severity” by itself, but we can implement a “break-glass” role for oncall, and policy/process says you only use it for Sev1+. Then we rely on audit + monitoring to catch misuse.

Marcus Lin: “break-glass” is fine if time-bound and heavily audited. I’d prefer we keep it narrow: rollback only (not apply/promote). Also add a cooldown so you can’t thrash.

Aisha Bello: Cooldown makes sense. But please don’t block multiple rollbacks if the first rollback doesn’t fully propagate (mixed worker state). Runbook has step “verify active version stabilized.”

Ethan Park: Runtime applier is idempotent now (PR-19918) and we fixed the apply/rollback race (PR-20111). Still, we should prevent rapid flip-flopping unless you explicitly override.

Sean Gallagher: Proposal:
1) Default: Only “Optimization Change Operator” can rollback (day-to-day).
2) Add role “SRE Oncall (break-glass)” that grants `optimization_config.rollback` for Hosted/Dedicated.
3) Policy: break-glass use only during Sev0/Sev1 or upon IC request.
4) Console/API require `reason` and `incident_id` when actor has break-glass role.
5) Audit event emitted always.

Paula Mendes: Works for me as long as for customers (tenant admins) it’s separate. They can only rollback within their tenant scope anyway.

Jordan Lee: Yep, customer admins get tenant-scoped permission in their org. This thread is about internal Redwood on-call.

Chloe Martin: One nuance: console needs to show the rollback button for oncall even if they’re not “optimization operator.” So RBAC needs to be clean; no hidden “admin” flags.

Naomi Feldman: What about Dedicated accounts with customer-managed oncall contacts? We don’t want Redwood SRE rolling back without notifying them.

Sean Gallagher: For Dedicated: runbook already says “notify customer on-call channel if Sev1+ and mitigation impacts config.” But permission-wise it’s the same (still Redwood control plane).

Marcus Lin: Add to runbook: for Dedicated/Private, ensure you have correct account + scope. We’ve had mis-scope incidents (wrong region) before.

Aisha Bello: Yep. Mis-scope is the big fear. Can the UI show blast radius summary (tenants/models/regions count) before confirm?

Chloe Martin: Yup, that’s in DES-213 copy: “This will revert X tenants / Y models / Z regions to version vNNN.” Also we can highlight if rollback target is not “promoted” / not last known-good.

Connor O'Brien: On-call workflow ask: Can incident bot post the runbook link + shortcut during incident channel creation?

Incident Bot: Noted. There is an open change request to add a rollback shortcut command for optimization config incidents.

Ethan Park: That’s PR-20081 in incident-bot, I think. Adds `/opt-rollback` to link Console + runbook.

Jordan Lee: Implementation detail: break-glass role in policy bundle should require MFA (SSO) and be assigned via PagerDuty oncall group membership sync (so it’s automatic for whoever is on primary).

Marcus Lin: +1. Also require session re-auth for rollback action (recent login) if possible.

Chloe Martin: We can do “re-auth” through SSO step-up for sensitive actions; might be phase 2 but we can at least require “type-to-confirm” + incident ID at GA.

Naomi Feldman: For audit: please ensure we log BOTH: user intent (reason, incident_id) and system context (scope, previous_version, new_version, rollout_state, request_id).

Ethan Park: We’re already emitting `optimization_config.rollback_requested` and `optimization_config.rollback_completed` with those fields in the audit spec. Will double-check the payload includes `actor_role` and `authz_mode` (normal vs break-glass).

Aisha Bello: Can we add a sanity check: rollback must target a version that was previously “active” for that scope (or “promoted”) unless override? That prevents rolling back to some random bad experiment.

Jordan Lee: Good guardrail. We can allow override only for Optimization Change Operator (not break-glass), or require second approval.

Sean Gallagher: +1. For oncall break-glass, keep it simple: rollback to last known-good / last promoted version only.

Connor O'Brien: That also helps support: “roll back to last promoted” is easy to explain.

Paula Mendes: Decision check:
- SRE oncall gets rollback permission via break-glass role
- limited to rollback-to-last-promoted for that scope
- must enter incident_id + reason
- audit logs include role + request_id

Sean Gallagher: Yep. I’ll update runbook with:
- Who can execute (Optimization Change Operator; SRE Oncall break-glass for Sev0/Sev1)
- Required fields (incident_id, reason)
- Dedicated comms step
- “rollback target must be last promoted” rule

Marcus Lin: Add an explicit “Post-incident review: validate break-glass usage” step. Security will sample these.

Jordan Lee: I’ll take action item to update RBAC bundle + PD sync note (ENG-4826/PM-902 follow-up).

Chloe Martin: I’ll confirm console confirmation modal enforces incident_id for break-glass actors and shows blast radius summary.

Aisha Bello: I’ll add an oncall checklist step: after rollback, verify active version header in responses + confirm p95 trend reversal on dashboard.

Sean Gallagher: Cool, thanks. I’m marking this as the decision unless objections in next day.

Connor O'Brien: No objections. Please ping me when the runbook update lands; I’ll link it in release train notes.

Incident Bot: Reminder set by Sean Gallagher: “Review break-glass rollback usage policy in 1 week.”

Jordan Lee: RBAC currently has `optimization_config.rollback` permission planned under the “optimization change operator” bundle (PM-902 / PR-20070). We can add a second role for SRE oncall / incident commander.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: rollback is a production change. I’m okay with SRE oncall having rollback *if*:
- scoped to Hosted/Dedicated only (control plane managed)
- requires reason + incident ID (or at least change ticket)
- emits audit event w/ request_id + actor + previous_version + target_version + scope
- optional: 2-person rule for Sev0/Sev1 (approve + execute)

Ethan Park: On the API side we can require `reason` + `actor_context` and propagate `incident_id` into audit payload. Console already planned to do “type-to-confirm”.

Chloe Martin: Console UX can enforce “type ROLLBACK + tenant/model/region scope” and require selecting a prior known-good version. But RBAC is the real gate.

Connor O'Brien: Release eng concern: we’ll get paged for latency regressions during release trains too. If rollback requires a special person, it’ll slow MTTR. But also we don’t want casual rollbacks for non-incidents.

Naomi Feldman: Can we distinguish:
- Sev0/Sev1 incident mitigation (fast path)
- non-incident change (normal approvals)

Jordan Lee: Yes. RBAC can’t read “incident severity” by itself, but we can implement a “break-glass” role for oncall, and policy/process says you only use it for Sev1+. Then we rely on audit + monitoring to catch misuse.

Marcus Lin: “break-glass” is fine if time-bound and heavily audited. I’d prefer we keep it narrow: rollback only (not apply/promote). Also add a cooldown so you can’t thrash.

Aisha Bello: Cooldown makes sense. But please don’t block multiple rollbacks if the first rollback doesn’t fully propagate (mixed worker state). Runbook has step “verify active version stabilized.”

Ethan Park: Runtime applier is idempotent now (PR-19918) and we fixed the apply/rollback race (PR-20111). Still, we should prevent rapid flip-flopping unless you explicitly override.

Sean Gallagher: Proposal:
1) Default: Only “Optimization Change Operator” can rollback (day-to-day).
2) Add role “SRE Oncall (break-glass)” that grants `optimization_config.rollback` for Hosted/Dedicated.
3) Policy: break-glass use only during Sev0/Sev1 or upon IC request.
4) Console/API require `reason` and `incident_id` when actor has break-glass role.
5) Audit event emitted always.

Paula Mendes: Works for me as long as for customers (tenant admins) it’s separate. They can only rollback within their tenant scope anyway.

Jordan Lee: Yep, customer admins get tenant-scoped permission in their org. This thread is about internal Redwood on-call.

Chloe Martin: One nuance: console needs to show the rollback button for oncall even if they’re not “optimization operator.” So RBAC needs to be clean; no hidden “admin” flags.

Naomi Feldman: What about Dedicated accounts with customer-managed oncall contacts? We don’t want Redwood SRE rolling back without notifying them.

Sean Gallagher: For Dedicated: runbook already says “notify customer on-call channel if Sev1+ and mitigation impacts config.” But permission-wise it’s the same (still Redwood control plane).

Marcus Lin: Add to runbook: for Dedicated/Private, ensure you have correct account + scope. We’ve had mis-scope incidents (wrong region) before.

Aisha Bello: Yep. Mis-scope is the big fear. Can the UI show blast radius summary (tenants/models/regions count) before confirm?

Chloe Martin: Yup, that’s in DES-213 copy: “This will revert X tenants / Y models / Z regions to version vNNN.” Also we can highlight if rollback target is not “promoted” / not last known-good.

Connor O'Brien: On-call workflow ask: Can incident bot post the runbook link + shortcut during incident channel creation?

Incident Bot: Noted. There is an open change request to add a rollback shortcut command for optimization config incidents.

Ethan Park: That’s PR-20081 in incident-bot, I think. Adds `/opt-rollback` to link Console + runbook.

Jordan Lee: Implementation detail: break-glass role in policy bundle should require MFA (SSO) and be assigned via PagerDuty oncall group membership sync (so it’s automatic for whoever is on primary).

Marcus Lin: +1. Also require session re-auth for rollback action (recent login) if possible.

Chloe Martin: We can do “re-auth” through SSO step-up for sensitive actions; might be phase 2 but we can at least require “type-to-confirm” + incident ID at GA.

Naomi Feldman: For audit: please ensure we log BOTH: user intent (reason, incident_id) and system context (scope, previous_version, new_version, rollout_state, request_id).

Ethan Park: We’re already emitting `optimization_config.rollback_requested` and `optimization_config.rollback_completed` with those fields in the audit spec. Will double-check the payload includes `actor_role` and `authz_mode` (normal vs break-glass).

Aisha Bello: Can we add a sanity check: rollback must target a version that was previously “active” for that scope (or “promoted”) unless override? That prevents rolling back to some random bad experiment.

Jordan Lee: Good guardrail. We can allow override only for Optimization Change Operator (not break-glass), or require second approval.

Sean Gallagher: +1. For oncall break-glass, keep it simple: rollback to last known-good / last promoted version only.

Connor O'Brien: That also helps support: “roll back to last promoted” is easy to explain.

Paula Mendes: Decision check:
- SRE oncall gets rollback permission via break-glass role
- limited to rollback-to-last-promoted for that scope
- must enter incident_id + reason
- audit logs include role + request_id

Sean Gallagher: Yep. I’ll update runbook with:
- Who can execute (Optimization Change Operator; SRE Oncall break-glass for Sev0/Sev1)
- Required fields (incident_id, reason)
- Dedicated comms step
- “rollback target must be last promoted” rule

Marcus Lin: Add an explicit “Post-incident review: validate break-glass usage” step. Security will sample these.

Jordan Lee: I’ll take action item to update RBAC bundle + PD sync note (ENG-4826/PM-902 follow-up).

Chloe Martin: I’ll confirm console confirmation modal enforces incident_id for break-glass actors and shows blast radius summary.

Aisha Bello: I’ll add an oncall checklist step: after rollback, verify active version header in responses + confirm p95 trend reversal on dashboard.

Sean Gallagher: Cool, thanks. I’m marking this as the decision unless objections in next day.

Connor O'Brien: No objections. Please ping me when the runbook update lands; I’ll link it in release train notes.

Incident Bot: Reminder set by Sean Gallagher: “Review break-glass rollback usage policy in 1 week.”

title: #eng-oncall: 1738096504
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Heads up: automated restore drill in staging failed again. This is the scheduled "private-dr-restore-drill" run that kicked off ~10 min ago. Can someone help triage? I’m oncall and trying to figure out if this is a real regression vs the usual staging flake.

Private-Installer Bot: Restore drill run failed
- env: staging
- cluster: priv-stg-usw2-02
- workflow: restore-drill-2025-01-28
- step: post-restore validation
- exit_code: 2
- logs: https://grafana.staging.redwood.ai/explore?orgId=1&left=%5B%22now-3h%22,%22now%22,%22loki%22,%7B%22expr%22:%22%7Bcluster%3D%5C%22priv-stg-usw2-02%5C%22,app%3D%5C%22private-installer%5C%22%7D%20%7C%3D%20%5C%22restore%20apply%5C%22%22%7D%5D
- artifacts: s3://redwood-private-staging-backups/restore-drills/2025-01-28/restore-drill-2025-01-28/ (kms key alias: alias/redwood-staging-backups)

Rafael Mendes: Is this the same failure mode as last week (missing CRDs / helm ordering)? Or something new?

Sean Gallagher: Not sure yet. Quick glance: looks like restore job completes, then validation complains about routing policies not present.

Sean Gallagher: Here’s the tail from the installer pod:
```text
INFO restore: applying backup manifest format=v1.2.0
INFO restore: restoring component=control-plane-db
INFO restore: restoring component=config
INFO restore: restoring component=helm-releases
WARN k8s: waiting for CRDs to be established…
INFO validate: checking tenant config + routing policies
ERROR validate: GET /control-plane/api/v1/routing/policies -> 404
ERROR validate: expected 6 policies, found 0
FATAL restore: post-restore validation failed
```

Sergio Costa: ^ 404 from control-plane API smells like control plane not ready (or hitting wrong svc) vs actual missing data. Do we have the trace/span for the validation request?

Sean Gallagher: Not yet. The explore link above should have spans but I didn’t dig.

Sergio Costa: Found it. Validation is calling `https://redwood-control-plane.redwood-private.svc:8443` (internal) and getting 404 from the router, not a connection error. That usually means the API server is up but route isn’t registered.

Bruno Silva: Route not registered could happen if the CRDs didn’t apply before the helm releases restore. We changed the helm hook ordering in pr-138 recently; did staging pick that up?

Sean Gallagher: Staging is on `private-installer 0.19.3` and charts `redwood-private 1.14.1` I think. Not sure if that includes pr-138.

Connor O'Brien: pr-138 landed in charts `1.14.2` (not in 1.14.1). If the restore is expecting the new hook, staging might be in an in-between state.

Benji Okafor: Another angle: does the backup include routing policies in this environment? We intentionally excluded some generated resources. But we should still restore the policy objects in the control-plane DB.

Michael Nguyen: If policies are DB-backed, 0 policies after restore suggests either (a) the DB restore didn’t happen / restored empty, or (b) we restored but control plane is pointing at a fresh DB.

Hanae Suzuki: Any KMS errors? If the DB snapshot decrypt failed, we might be falling back to "init new" silently (bad). Check for `envelope decrypt` logs.

Sean Gallagher: Good call. Grepping logs… I see:
```text
INFO crypto: using provider=kms key_alias=alias/redwood-staging-backups
INFO crypto: decrypted DEK ok
```
So probably not that.

Sergio Costa: In Loki I see something earlier:
```text
ERROR k8s: (Forbidden) cannot list resource "customresourcedefinitions" in API group "apiextensions.k8s.io" at the cluster scope
```
Looks like RBAC on the restore job SA.

Bruno Silva: Oh wow. That would explain CRDs not being present/verified, then helm restore proceeds and API routes never come up correctly.

Connor O'Brien: That matches INT-612-ish from a different thread. Did we fix RBAC in chart values or was it manual?

Bruno Silva: We fixed RBAC in helm chart pr-131 (backup cronjob RBAC) and pr-138 (restore job template). If staging is still on 1.14.1, it likely doesn’t have the CRD list permission for the restore Job.

Rafael Mendes: So likely not a product regression, but staging running old chart version vs restore drill expecting new behavior.

Sean Gallagher: What’s the right immediate action? Disable paging for this drill until we upgrade charts? Or hotfix RBAC in staging?

Benji Okafor: For oncall sanity: I’d hotfix RBAC in staging now so the drill signal is meaningful. Then file ticket to ensure drill uses pinned compatible versions.

Bruno Silva: I can patch the clusterrole + binding quickly. Minimal:
- allow `get,list,watch` on CRDs
- allow `get,list` on `validatingwebhookconfigurations` too (we’ve hit that before)
I’ll DM you the yaml.

Hanae Suzuki: +1 to patching. Also please confirm restore job SA name. Some clusters use `redwood-private-restore` vs `redwood-private-installer`.

Sean Gallagher: SA in logs is `redwood-private-restore`. If you have the exact manifests, I’ll apply.

Bruno Silva: Here you go (apply to `redwood-private` namespace). This is what’s in 1.14.2:
```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: redwood-private-restore
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apiextensions.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["customresourcedefinitions"]
  verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
- apiGroups: ["admissionregistration.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["validatingwebhookconfigurations","mutatingwebhookconfigurations"]
  verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: redwood-private-restore
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: redwood-private-restore
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: redwood-private-restore
  namespace: redwood-private
```

Sean Gallagher: Applied. Re-running the workflow manually now.

Sergio Costa: While you rerun, I’ll add a note to the dashboard that drill failures should be ignored on 1.14.1. Also we should update the alert to include chart version label once we have it.

Rafael Mendes: Please capture this in the incident-style thread too so we don’t lose it. Action item: upgrade staging charts to >=1.14.2 and align installer expectations.

Michael Nguyen: One more thing: if CRD list was forbidden, we should ensure the restore script fails early (preflight) instead of limping into validation. Might be worth adding a guard.

Connor O'Brien: Agreed. I’ll open a follow-up in release checklist: staging must run same chart version as drill harness, otherwise we get noisy pages.

Sean Gallagher: Rerun result: restore/validation passed. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Summary for posterity: failure was RBAC on `redwood-private-restore` SA preventing CRD list/watch; led to missing route registration + validation 404s. Patched ClusterRole/Binding (same as charts 1.14.2) and rerun succeeded.

Rafael Mendes: Nice. Sean can you drop the Loki snippet + patch into the incidents thread too? I’ll link it to ENG-9827/9822 as a hardening item (fail fast on RBAC).

Sean Gallagher: Will do.

title: #eng-oncall: 1737857788
preview:
Aisha Bello: hey all, oncall q re unit-econ backfill. we need to backfill missing hours from yesterday (looks like ingestion lag). what’s the safest way to run the backfill job without nuking the warehouse? also what are the “blessed” verification queries to confirm it worked?

Aisha Bello: context: grafana alert fired for completeness < 99% on `unit_econ_hourly` for ~6 hours. i can run the backfill tool but want to make sure I’m not stepping on prod agg.

Sean Gallagher: +1, please do NOT run the full quarter backfill ad-hoc lol. we’ve had warehouse timeouts from that.

Mei Lin: safest path:
1) confirm the gap window (exact hours, region(s)?)
2) run backfill only for that window + throttle
3) verify counts vs raw metering + the DQ check view

Mei Lin: the backfill tool should be idempotent (upserts by hour/model/region/tier/quant profile). but you still want to keep concurrency low.

Aisha Bello: do we have the exact command line / args? i’m seeing a few scripts in `analytics/jobs/` but don’t want to guess.

Logan Wright: use the `unit-econ-agg` backfill entrypoint. it’s what ENG-8433/PR-19412 added. if you’re oncall you should have perms in the prod runner.

Logan Wright: rough shape is:
```bash
# runs in k8s job runner
unit-econ-agg backfill \
  --env=prod \
  --start=2025-01-25T04:00:00Z \
  --end=2025-01-25T10:00:00Z \
  --granularity=hour \
  --max-partitions=2 \
  --sleep-between-partitions=30s \
  --dry-run=false
```
^^ adjust window + keep `max-partitions` small (1-2).

Allison Grant: also: check if the hourly scheduled job has caught up first. a lot of the time it’s just delayed and will fill itself. if it’s still behind, backfill.

Aisha Bello: good call. what’s the “caught up” definition? the alert is “freshness > 90m” + “completeness”.

Caleb Johnson: for economics pipeline:
- freshness: latest `hour_bucket` in `unit_econ_hourly` should be >= now() - 2 hours (we allow one hour lag + processing)
- completeness: for each hour we expect coverage for required dims (region, tier) above threshold. the DQ view summarizes.

Caleb Johnson: if freshness is red but completeness is ok for the hours present, it’s usually ingestion lag. if completeness is red for a specific hour, it’s often a dimension mapping issue (region null, tier unknown) dropping rows.

Aisha Bello: ok. so before backfill, check warehouse to see which hours missing. can someone paste the query you use?

Mei Lin: yep. for “what hours are missing”:
```sql
-- hours present in the last 24h
select
  hour_bucket,
  sum(output_tokens) as output_tokens,
  sum(total_cost_usd) as total_cost_usd
from analytics.unit_econ_hourly
where hour_bucket >= dateadd('hour', -24, date_trunc('hour', current_timestamp()))
group by 1
order by 1;
```

Mei Lin: and to find gaps, compare to a generated series (depends on warehouse flavor). we have a helper table in prod:
```sql
select h.hour_bucket
from analytics.util_hours h
left join (
  select distinct hour_bucket
  from analytics.unit_econ_hourly
  where hour_bucket >= dateadd('hour', -24, date_trunc('hour', current_timestamp()))
) e using (hour_bucket)
where h.hour_bucket >= dateadd('hour', -24, date_trunc('hour', current_timestamp()))
  and e.hour_bucket is null
order by 1;
```

Aisha Bello: nice. what about verifying the backfill didn’t double count? (since it’s upsert-ish)

Mei Lin: backfill writes by unique key; still verify by checking that aggregates for the hour match expected totals and are stable across rerun.

Mei Lin: you can also rerun in `--dry-run=true` first to see “would write N rows”. then run real. then run dry-run again; it should show 0 writes.

Logan Wright: +1 to dry-run sanity check.

Aisha Bello: do we have a dry-run output example? (i want to know what to look for)

Logan Wright: usually logs like:
```text
backfill window: 2025-01-25T04:00:00Z..2025-01-25T10:00:00Z (6 partitions)
partition 1/6: hour=2025-01-25T04:00:00Z
computed rows: 4,812
would upsert rows: 4,812
...
summary: computed=28,941 would_upsert=28,941
```
post-run dry-run should show `would_upsert=0` (or tiny if late-arriving raw data changed).

Caleb Johnson: verification queries (post-backfill):

Caleb Johnson: 1) DQ summary for the window:
```sql
select
  hour_bucket,
  completeness_score,
  missing_region_pct,
  missing_tier_pct,
  notes
from analytics.unit_econ_data_quality_hourly
where hour_bucket >= '2025-01-25T04:00:00Z'
  and hour_bucket <  '2025-01-25T10:00:00Z'
order by 1;
```

Caleb Johnson: 2) compare raw metering output tokens vs aggregated output tokens (should be within tolerance; small diff if exclusions apply):
```sql
with raw as (
  select
    date_trunc('hour', ts) as hour_bucket,
    sum(output_tokens) as raw_output_tokens
  from analytics.metering_requests
  where ts >= '2025-01-25T04:00:00Z'
    and ts <  '2025-01-25T10:00:00Z'
    and metric_source = 'gateway'
  group by 1
), agg as (
  select
    hour_bucket,
    sum(output_tokens) as agg_output_tokens
  from analytics.unit_econ_hourly
  where hour_bucket >= '2025-01-25T04:00:00Z'
    and hour_bucket <  '2025-01-25T10:00:00Z'
  group by 1
)
select
  coalesce(raw.hour_bucket, agg.hour_bucket) as hour_bucket,
  raw_output_tokens,
  agg_output_tokens,
  (agg_output_tokens - raw_output_tokens) as diff,
  (agg_output_tokens / nullif(raw_output_tokens,0)) as ratio
from raw
full outer join agg using (hour_bucket)
order by 1;
```

Caleb Johnson: if ratio is wildly off (like <0.95 or >1.05) for one hour, stop and ask — it’s likely not “needs backfill” but “dimension drop” or metering issue.

Nadia Rahman: +1 and if it’s streaming heavy hours, check streaming parity. we had that undercount bug recently. make sure you’re comparing the right raw source (gateway vs runtime). if you see a gap only on streaming, ping me.

Aisha Bello: got it. for “not overloading the warehouse” besides max-partitions, any other knobs?

Sean Gallagher: run in the maintenance window if possible (we usually do after 02:00 UTC). if urgent, keep it small + watch warehouse queue depth.

Sean Gallagher: if you see query timeouts / elevated latency in warehouse, abort and re-run later with `--max-partitions=1` and larger sleep.

1) check if scheduler caught up (freshness)

from analytics.unit_econ_hourly

Aisha Bello: context: grafana alert fired for completeness < 99% on `unit_econ_hourly` for ~6 hours. i can run the backfill tool but want to make sure I’m not stepping on prod agg.

Sean Gallagher: +1, please do NOT run the full quarter backfill ad-hoc lol. we’ve had warehouse timeouts from that.

Mei Lin: safest path:
1) confirm the gap window (exact hours, region(s)?)
2) run backfill only for that window + throttle
3) verify counts vs raw metering + the DQ check view

Mei Lin: the backfill tool should be idempotent (upserts by hour/model/region/tier/quant profile). but you still want to keep concurrency low.

Aisha Bello: do we have the exact command line / args? i’m seeing a few scripts in `analytics/jobs/` but don’t want to guess.

Logan Wright: use the `unit-econ-agg` backfill entrypoint. it’s what ENG-8433/PR-19412 added. if you’re oncall you should have perms in the prod runner.

Logan Wright: rough shape is:
```bash
# runs in k8s job runner
unit-econ-agg backfill \
  --env=prod \
  --start=2025-01-25T04:00:00Z \
  --end=2025-01-25T10:00:00Z \
  --granularity=hour \
  --max-partitions=2 \
  --sleep-between-partitions=30s \
  --dry-run=false
```
^^ adjust window + keep `max-partitions` small (1-2).

Allison Grant: also: check if the hourly scheduled job has caught up first. a lot of the time it’s just delayed and will fill itself. if it’s still behind, backfill.

Aisha Bello: good call. what’s the “caught up” definition? the alert is “freshness > 90m” + “completeness”.

Caleb Johnson: for economics pipeline:
- freshness: latest `hour_bucket` in `unit_econ_hourly` should be >= now() - 2 hours (we allow one hour lag + processing)
- completeness: for each hour we expect coverage for required dims (region, tier) above threshold. the DQ view summarizes.

Caleb Johnson: if freshness is red but completeness is ok for the hours present, it’s usually ingestion lag. if completeness is red for a specific hour, it’s often a dimension mapping issue (region null, tier unknown) dropping rows.

Aisha Bello: ok. so before backfill, check warehouse to see which hours missing. can someone paste the query you use?

Mei Lin: yep. for “what hours are missing”:
```sql
-- hours present in the last 24h
select
  hour_bucket,
  sum(output_tokens) as output_tokens,
  sum(total_cost_usd) as total_cost_usd
from analytics.unit_econ_hourly
where hour_bucket >= dateadd('hour', -24, date_trunc('hour', current_timestamp()))
group by 1
order by 1;
```

Mei Lin: and to find gaps, compare to a generated series (depends on warehouse flavor). we have a helper table in prod:
```sql
select h.hour_bucket
from analytics.util_hours h
left join (
  select distinct hour_bucket
  from analytics.unit_econ_hourly
  where hour_bucket >= dateadd('hour', -24, date_trunc('hour', current_timestamp()))
) e using (hour_bucket)
where h.hour_bucket >= dateadd('hour', -24, date_trunc('hour', current_timestamp()))
  and e.hour_bucket is null
order by 1;
```

Aisha Bello: nice. what about verifying the backfill didn’t double count? (since it’s upsert-ish)

Mei Lin: backfill writes by unique key; still verify by checking that aggregates for the hour match expected totals and are stable across rerun.

Mei Lin: you can also rerun in `--dry-run=true` first to see “would write N rows”. then run real. then run dry-run again; it should show 0 writes.

Logan Wright: +1 to dry-run sanity check.

Aisha Bello: do we have a dry-run output example? (i want to know what to look for)

Logan Wright: usually logs like:
```text
backfill window: 2025-01-25T04:00:00Z..2025-01-25T10:00:00Z (6 partitions)
partition 1/6: hour=2025-01-25T04:00:00Z
computed rows: 4,812
would upsert rows: 4,812
...
summary: computed=28,941 would_upsert=28,941
```
post-run dry-run should show `would_upsert=0` (or tiny if late-arriving raw data changed).

Caleb Johnson: verification queries (post-backfill):

Caleb Johnson: 1) DQ summary for the window:
```sql
select
  hour_bucket,
  completeness_score,
  missing_region_pct,
  missing_tier_pct,
  notes
from analytics.unit_econ_data_quality_hourly
where hour_bucket >= '2025-01-25T04:00:00Z'
  and hour_bucket <  '2025-01-25T10:00:00Z'
order by 1;
```

Caleb Johnson: 2) compare raw metering output tokens vs aggregated output tokens (should be within tolerance; small diff if exclusions apply):
```sql
with raw as (
  select
    date_trunc('hour', ts) as hour_bucket,
    sum(output_tokens) as raw_output_tokens
  from analytics.metering_requests
  where ts >= '2025-01-25T04:00:00Z'
    and ts <  '2025-01-25T10:00:00Z'
    and metric_source = 'gateway'
  group by 1

  select
    hour_bucket,
    sum(output_tokens) as agg_output_tokens
  from analytics.unit_econ_hourly
  where hour_bucket >= '2025-01-25T04:00:00Z'
    and hour_bucket <  '2025-01-25T10:00:00Z'
  group by 1
)
select
  coalesce(raw.hour_bucket, agg.hour_bucket) as hour_bucket,
  raw_output_tokens,
  agg_output_tokens,
  (agg_output_tokens - raw_output_tokens) as diff,
  (agg_output_tokens / nullif(raw_output_tokens,0)) as ratio
from raw
full outer join agg using (hour_bucket)
order by 1;
```

Allison Grant: we also have an oncall checklist item: announce in #eng-platform when running a backfill so others don’t kick off heavy jobs simultaneously.

Logan Wright: generally no. the hourly job processes “latest hour” and backfill is historical. they can coexist. the only time we pause is if the job is thrashing and reprocessing the same window (rare).

Mei Lin: if you backfill a window that overlaps the scheduler’s current processing hour, you can get contention. safest: backfill only completed hours (e.g., end <= now - 2h).

Aisha Bello: makes sense.

Aisha Bello: plan:

2) run gap query
3) dry-run backfill for exact missing hours (max-partitions=1-2)
4) run real backfill
5) rerun DQ + raw vs agg ratio query
6) post in #eng-platform and resolve alert

Aisha Bello: anything else before i do it?

Caleb Johnson: add one more quick check: ensure tier/region coverage isn’t dropping (it’ll cause completeness alert even if tokens exist).
```sql
select
  hour_bucket,
  sum(case when region = 'unknown' then output_tokens else 0 end) / nullif(sum(output_tokens),0) as unknown_region_token_share,
  sum(case when customer_tier = 'unknown' then output_tokens else 0 end) / nullif(sum(output_tokens),0) as unknown_tier_token_share
from analytics.unit_econ_hourly
where hour_bucket >= '2025-01-25T04:00:00Z'
  and hour_bucket <  '2025-01-25T10:00:00Z'
group by 1
order by 1;
```
If unknown shares spike, don’t backfill—open incident for mapping.

Aisha Bello: perfect. thanks all. kicking off checks now.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please update thread when done + whether alert clears.

Aisha Bello: update: scheduler still missing 05:00-08:00. running dry-run for 05-09 now with max-partitions=1.

Aisha Bello: dry-run shows would_upsert ~19k rows total. proceeding with real run.

Aisha Bello: post-run: DQ view now shows completeness_score back to 0.998+ for those hours, raw vs agg ratio ~0.99-1.01. alerts recovering.

Allison Grant: nice. can you drop a note in #eng-platform for visibility?

Aisha Bello: done. also filed a follow-up to tune throttling defaults; the job was fine but queries were heavy for ~5m.

title: #eng-oncall: 1737506650
preview:
Ivy Chen: oncall q: how do we *validate audit log delivery* during a partial region outage? like us-east degraded but us-west ok. i want something more concrete than “check dashboards are green” before we tell Support/customers. what’s the current playbook?

Datadog: [ALERT] audit-ingest - delivery_latency_p95 breached (region=us-east-1, env=prod) | p95=92s threshold=60s

Sean Gallagher: good q. we don’t have a single “push button” validation yet, but there are a couple checks that work together.

Sean Gallagher: first: confirm whether we’re in “ingest backlog” vs “producer not emitting” vs “exporter blocked”. region outages can look like all 3 depending on which component is impacted.

Ivy Chen: yeah. specifically: if us-east is partially down, requests may still be served from us-west. how do we prove the audit events for those actions still make it to the sink + exports?

Rafael Mendes: runbook draft covers this, but it’s a bit buried. linking: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Audit+Log+Pipeline+Incident+Runbook (section “Regional impairment / failover validation”).

Kevin Osei: from exporter side: delivery is at-least-once. during region flaps you might see duplicates. validation should focus on: (1) events still arriving (2) no sustained drops (3) latency trending back down once region recovers.

Allison Grant: +1. also make sure you’re looking at the right graphs. audit pipeline is split:
- producer emit (service logs / counters)
- ingest accepted (gateway -> audit ingest)
- queue/buffer depth
- exporter success/error per target

Logan Wright: we’ve got the “expected vs observed” view in the coverage dashboard but it’s not perfect for real-time outage validation. still useful to sanity check that the sensitive event types are continuing to show up.

Ivy Chen: can someone give a concrete step list? like: “perform action X in region Y” and then “search for event_id Z”

Amara Diallo: for hosted/dedicated you can do a safe synthetic: hit the admin test endpoint? we don’t have a dedicated “audit test event” endpoint yet.

Sean Gallagher: we intentionally avoided a public “emit arbitrary audit event” endpoint. but we can do a low-risk sensitive action in a sandbox tenant.

Benji Okafor: easiest RBAC action in a non-prod tenant: create a temp role and delete it. that should emit:
- rbac.role.create
- rbac.role.delete
and it exercises actor + target + diff fields.

Nadia Rahman: if you’re validating *region failover*, make sure the request actually routes to the alternate region. in prod, you can force region via header in internal tooling, or do it from a host that prefers us-west. otherwise you may think you’re testing us-west but still hitting us-east.

Jordan Lee: for policy changes (also sensitive) there’s usually more guardrails. RBAC change in an internal test tenant is better.

Ivy Chen: ok, so:
1) do RBAC create/delete in sandbox tenant
2) confirm event shows up
but where do i look to confirm it “made it” end-to-end?

Kevin Osei: if you have access to the raw audit stream (internal), quickest is querying ingest store by correlation IDs.
- grab request_id / trace_id from the API response headers or service logs
- search audit-events index for request_id

Kevin Osei: if you’re validating SIEM export specifically, pick one configured internal Splunk HEC target (we have the staging one) and check the exporter “delivered_events_total{target=splunk_hec}” increments + sample payload includes event_id.

Rafael Mendes: for oncall, we usually do:
A) dashboards: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/audit-pipeline/audit-log-pipeline (latency, drop rate, queue depth by region)
B) spot check: search audit event store for the synthetic action by request_id
C) exporter check: exporter success rate + if customer is impacted, have Support ask for a sample of received events and verify event_id continuity.

Allison Grant: and for partial region outage, the key is comparing producer emits vs ingest accepts.
- if producer emits keep increasing but ingest accepts flatlines in us-east -> ingest path issue
- if both flatline -> requests may not be hitting that region or producers failing

Sean Gallagher: concrete “do this now” (draft, until we formalize):
1. Pick internal sandbox tenant ("sec-audit-sandbox").
2. From a host that routes to us-west (or set internal routing override), call control-plane RBAC create role. Immediately delete role.
3. Capture request_id from response headers.
4. Query audit-events index: `request_id:<id>` and confirm you see the two events with `schema_version=v1`.
5. Confirm `ingest_accepted_total` increased in us-west and `drop_rate` not elevated.
6. If validating SIEM export: confirm exporter `delivered_events_total` increases and the payload contains the same `event_id` values. Remind: duplicates possible; dedupe by event_id.

Connor O'Brien: re: routing override, internal doc: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/internal/routing/region-override (works for control-plane + console). might be stale though.

Ivy Chen: is there a “known good” way to query audit-events index? i don’t have the exact kibana/grafana panel handy.

Logan Wright: use the “Audit Events Search” saved view in OpenSearch Dashboards: `prod-audit-events-*`. filter fields:
- `tenant_id` = sec-audit-sandbox
- `event_type` = rbac.role.create OR rbac.role.delete
- `request_id` = ...

Logan Wright: if you don’t see it there but producer counters increment, it’s an ingest problem. if you see it there but customer export is missing, it’s exporter/target.

Rafael Mendes: we should add this as a small explicit subsection in the runbook. I’ll update the draft with these steps + links to the saved view.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Ivy, in the meantime if you’re mid-incident: paste the request_id here and we can help confirm in the index.

Ivy Chen: cool. i’ll run the RBAC create/delete with west routing override and report back.

Ivy Chen: update: ran create/delete, got request_id `req_9b7c1a...`.

Kevin Osei: found both events in `prod-audit-events-*` with that request_id. `event_id` present, `schema_version=v1`, `region=us-west-2`, `delivery_status=accepted`.

Allison Grant: grafana shows ingest latency p95 back down to 18s in us-east now; queue depth draining. i’d call out “no drops observed, temporary latency increase” in incident notes.

Sean Gallagher: nice. for customer comms: be careful to not promise “exactly once”. we can say “at-least-once delivery; dedupe using event_id” if they ask.

Rafael Mendes: action item: I’ll PR the runbook change + add a short “partial region outage validation checklist” with these exact steps + links.

Ivy Chen: thanks all. this is what i needed.
